title: The Clockmaker's Fever
body: A 52-year-old female presents with a 3-day history of burning urination, urinary frequency, and flank pain. Urine culture grows E. coli, sensitive to multiple antibiotics. She has a history of epilepsy and takes valproate daily. She also has a G6PD deficiency diagnosed in childhood. Her eGFR is normal.
fennick_hint: Two of these options share a mechanism that becomes lethal in a patient whose red blood cells lack a certain protective enzyme. A third carries a hidden danger for a patient whose seizures are already under chemical control. Read every detail — the answer has already been given to you.

explanation.correct: Cephalexin covers E. coli UTI effectively, has no interaction with valproate, and is safe in G6PD deficiency. It is the clean first-line choice when all other options carry specific contraindications in this patient.

wrong.Ciprofloxacin: Fluoroquinolones lower the seizure threshold and can reduce valproate serum levels through CYP interactions — a dangerous combination in a patient with epilepsy already on anticonvulsant therapy.
wrong.Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole: Sulfonamides cause oxidative stress on red blood cells. In G6PD deficiency, the cell cannot neutralize this stress, triggering hemolytic anemia. Hard contraindication.
wrong.Nitrofurantoin: Also causes oxidative hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients through the same mechanism as sulfonamides. Both B and C are traps for the same reason — the G6PD deficiency catches them both.

title: The Merchant's Cough
body: A 67-year-old male presents with fever, productive cough, and consolidation on chest X-ray consistent with community-acquired pneumonia. He reports a severe penicillin allergy — anaphylaxis on prior exposure. He has CKD stage 3 and takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation. Sputum culture is pending.
fennick_hint: One option shares a family with a drug this patient has already proven he cannot tolerate. Two others belong to the same class — but only one of them carries a dangerous interaction with his current medications. Elimination, not guesswork.

explanation.correct: Levofloxacin covers both typical and atypical CAP organisms, is safe in penicillin allergy, and requires only dose adjustment for CKD stage 3 — not avoidance. It has no significant interaction with warfarin at standard doses.

wrong.Amoxicillin-clavulanate: This is a penicillin-class antibiotic. The patient had anaphylaxis to penicillin — an absolute contraindication. Cross-reactivity risk is real and this must be eliminated immediately.
wrong.Azithromycin: Azithromycin monotherapy is insufficient for CAP in an elderly patient with significant comorbidities. It also carries QT prolongation risk in a patient with AFib. Not the right choice here.
wrong.Clarithromycin: Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor. It would dramatically increase warfarin's anticoagulant effect in this patient, creating serious bleeding risk. This interaction is a clinical contraindication.

title: The Engineer's Chest
body: A 34-year-old male with no significant past medical history presents with a 5-day productive cough, fever of 38.8°C, and right lower lobe consolidation on X-ray. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Sputum Gram stain shows Gram-positive diplococci. He is hemodynamically stable and can be managed as an outpatient.
fennick_hint: When a patient offers you no contraindications, no allergies, and a clear organism — the answer is almost always the simplest one. Two of these options are reserved for resistant organisms or hospital settings. One covers atypicals but misses this organism's first-line treatment.

explanation.correct: Amoxicillin is the first-line outpatient treatment for CAP caused by S. pneumoniae (Gram-positive diplococci) in a young healthy patient with no allergies or comorbidities. No reason to escalate therapy.

wrong.Vancomycin: Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA, severe hospital-acquired infections, or patients who have failed other therapies. Using it for outpatient CAP in a healthy young adult is gross over-treatment and promotes resistance.
wrong.Meropenem: A broad-spectrum carbapenem is entirely inappropriate for a simple outpatient CAP. It is reserved for multi-drug resistant organisms and severe hospital infections. Massive antibiotic stewardship violation.
wrong.Doxycycline: Doxycycline is an alternative for atypical CAP coverage, but it is not the first-line agent for S. pneumoniae pneumonia. Amoxicillin has superior activity against this organism in a patient with no contraindications.
